heading: MODULUS SEXTUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the treatment of light (lux) within the celestial city, the first concentus of the sixth modus. Zorzi draws on Phoenician theology as reported by Iamblichus to argue that corporeal goods derive from the sun and planets, while the spiritual goods of the celestial citizens derive from the Pater luminum and the "Sun" who declared "Ego sum lux mundi" (Christ), with higher intellects mediating illumination downward in a hierarchy following Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite. Zorzi confesses the apophatic difficulty of the subject, noting that the supreme light blinded the unready eye and communicated itself to Solomon, the prophets, and Moses only through darkness (caligo). He introduces a Hebrew philological note — distinguishing karana (shining/radiant) from qeren (horn), arguing that the Vulgate error of Moses having "horns" descending Sinai arose from confusing the same Hebrew consonants with different vocalic pointing.
essay:
## Overview: Light as the Opening Theme of the Sixth Modus

Chunk 112 marks the transition within the *Modulus Sextus* from the ornament of the celestial city to the subject of light itself — the medium without which all ornament would be invisible. Zorzi announces the topic as "Lux / Quid sit lux in genere" (What light is in general), establishing it as the first *concentus* of this modus. The programmatic sentence is terse but structurally significant: "Post civitatis ornatum concinne admodum de luce pertractatur, qua videri possint ornamenta ipsa, et aspicientes delectari in eo, a quo omnia bona suscipiunt." Light is the condition of visibility and the source of all goods — the logic is both aesthetic and metaphysical.

## Phoenician Theology and the Iamblichan Hierarchy of Light

Zorzi grounds his account of light in an unexpected ancient source: the Phoenicians, as reported by Iamblichus. The passage runs:

> Sicut enim (ut docent Phoenices in sua theologia Iamblico referente) bona omnia corporea a luce solis absque ullo medio, aut a planetis et stellis, quibus ipsa lux Phoebea communicatur, suscipimus, sic in coelesti civitate quicquid boni habent cives illi, suscipiunt a Patre luminum, et ab illo Sole, qui ait: Ego sum lux mundi, aut a superioribus mentibus, qui fortiores illuminationes a suprema luce comparantes, inferiores postea illuminant.

<cite>p. 2544</cite>

The structure is a precise analogical argument: just as the sun radiates corporeal goods either directly or through the mediation of planets and stars, so in the celestial city all spiritual goods descend from the *Pater luminum* (a Jacobine phrase, Jas. 1:17), through Christ ("Ego sum lux mundi," Jn. 8:12), and through the higher celestial intellects that relay stronger illuminations to weaker ones. The source for this hierarchical mediation is explicitly named as Dionysius — Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite — "arcanorum illorum conscius," the one initiated into those mysteries. This places Zorzi squarely within the Dionysian tradition of graded illumination (*illuminatio*), which he harmonizes with the Iamblichan cosmological doctrine to produce a synthetic Neoplatonic-Christian framework.

## Apophatic Confession: The Paradox of the Dazzling Dark

Having announced the theme, Zorzi immediately enters an apophatic register. He confesses that light is the most obscure of all subjects precisely because of its excess of luminosity:

> primo intuitu perhorresco, quia obscurissimum omnium quid mihi sese offert, quod quidem quo magis splendet, eo etiam obscuriori caligine contrahitur, et densiori involvitur nebula, ut oculi non bene expurgati offendantur potius quam illustrentur.

<cite>p. 2544</cite>

This paradox — the brighter the light, the deeper the darkness it produces in unprepared eyes — is the classic Neoplatonic account of the intellect's incapacity before the One, here Christianized and applied to the *lux suprema*. Zorzi invokes the topos of divine *caligo* (darkness): the supreme light spoke to Solomon, the prophets, and Moses precisely through darkness, because their eyes had not yet been purged of lower shadows sufficiently to bear the radiance directly.

## Moses on Sinai: Hebrew Philology and the Vulgate Error

The passage culminates in a remarkable piece of Hebrew philological correction. Moses, when purified of corporeal impediments, transcended the *caligo* and saw the supreme light "facie ad faciem." Paul (2 Cor. 3:7) is cited for the report that the Israelites could not look upon Moses's face because of the splendor. But Zorzi then addresses the notorious Vulgate rendering of Moses's shining face as "cornuta" (horned):

> Hebraei enim splendentem dicunt (cid:687)G(cid:661)(cid:695) karana, et cornu (cid:687)9(cid:659)(cid:695) qeren eisdem quidem litteris, sed variatis punctis.

<cite>p. 2545</cite>

The Hebrew root *q-r-n* (קרן) can mean either "to be radiant/emit rays" or "horn," and the two meanings are distinguished only by vocalization (pointing). Zorzi correctly identifies this as the source of the Vulgate error: "qui error ex similitudine vocabuli ortus est." This is a direct engagement with the Hebrew text against the received Latin translation, consistent with Zorzi's broader Hebraist program throughout *De Harmonia Mundi* and with his use of Kabbalistic sources. The observation was not original to Zorzi — it circulates in humanist commentary — but its deployment here underscores that access to divine light requires philological as well as spiritual purification.

## The Veil and the Danger of Presumptuous Speech

The chunk closes with a warning about those who presume to speak of divine light without proper preparation:

> Multi sunt, qui nequeunt sufferre illam lucem, sicuti et ille vulgaris populus, unde necesse est, ut interponatur velamen, alioquin suis tenebris offensi non valentes comprehendere illam lucem, cum de ipsa loqui praesumunt, multa proferunt deliramenta.

<cite>p. 2545</cite>

The "velamen" (veil) is both Pauline (2 Cor. 3:13–14, the veil over Moses's face/the veil over the Old Testament for those who do not turn to Christ) and a general epistemological principle: those unprepared for the direct light need a mediating screen, and those who lack it but speak anyway produce nonsense. This prepares the reader for Zorzi's own more cautious, hieratic approach in the concentus that follow.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This opening concentus on light knits together several threads running through *De Harmonia Mundi*: (1) the Neoplatonic–Dionysian hierarchy of illumination; (2) the Christianization of Phoenician/Platonic solar theology via Iamblichus; (3) the apophatic tradition of *caligo* (the Dionysian dark cloud, the Mosaic *tenebra*); and (4) the Hebraist-Kabbalistic philological method that reads the Hebrew Bible against the Vulgate. The *Pater luminum* anchor connects to the Kabbalistic *Ein Sof* as source of all light and emanation, which Zorzi will develop in subsequent concentus. The celestial city, whose ornaments have just been detailed, is now revealed as a luminous space whose intelligibility depends entirely on this primordial light — a cosmological and theological claim simultaneously.
